speaker: interviewer
text: And this is tape two. OK, Peter--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --now just tell us a bit more. You were taken to-- to [? Munchauer-- ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: To Mukacevo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mukacevo, that's right. And to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And because there was such a big population there, it was-- must have been incredibly crowded.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very crowded. It was packed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how big was the-- the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know in size, but there were literally thousands of people there, from-- from memory. And I remember, vividly, one of my aunties coming over to me. And we still haven't heard what happened to my brother, and that upset us very much, because you can imagine, we didn't know what was-- where he is. And my father tried to find out and used to-- we always just waited and waited to hear. And he couldn't find out. He didn't know what happened. And then somebody-- I don't know how it came about-- somebody said they took him to the hospital. But I don't believe that that happened, because if they did, we would have-- we would have heard about it-- about it. And I tried after the war to find out, and there's no trace. And that's where I remember my auntie came along and, for the first time, she cut my payots off. She said you can't go with those, you know, because they were pull-- pulling them. They were just pulling beards and payots. You know, they were being very, very, very nasty.

speaker: interviewer
text: So life was very, very hard there, because we didn't know where we were, and-- and we were all together. And people were, as I said, on top of one another. So we-- So how many-- where did you stay? You know, what sort of accommodation did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no accommodation. You just had a little-- a little patch, one area, and your family was staying there. And then next to you was another family, another family. Toilet facilities were nonexistent. Washing was hardly-- hardly, you know, existing. So it was just like-- like dog-- like, sort of, you know, we were treated like not humans anymore. That was--

speaker: interviewer
text: But were they-- was it inside? In buildings? Or was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was in a building. So we stayed there for a few weeks, and the transports were going all the time from there. Trains and trains were leaving, and we didn't know where they were leaving. You see, the trains were going all the time. They were filling up trains-- the cattle trains, as you know-- as you know. And some of them said work camp. Others said somewhere in Poland, that we're going to Poland. Rumors were flying all the time, and everybody was listening. Everybody wanted to know where they're taking the trains. I mean, loads of trains-- chockablock. Trains were leaving there all the time with people. And nobody knew where they're going. It was only rumors.

speaker: interviewer
text: And rumors were rife. Every five minutes, you heard something else. Oh no, they're going to a nice farm. They're going to work, to nice places. No, they're going to horrible places. You-- you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was unsettling us. So we couldn't eat. We couldn't sleep. We were-- we were worried sick. And then, people were crying, and-- and when people were separated, you know, families, like, neighbors were taken-- oh, you know, we are going. And see, each one was going at different times. So it was a very awful, awful sort of a place. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you any food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there's some food. I don't know how it happened, but my father used to bring the food to us, you know? Where he got the food, I don't know. But I do know-- and I can still remember-- it's an interesting thing-- years later-- that my auntie cooked a big pot of food. And the smell of that food stayed with me for years and years and years. It was like a [NON-ENGLISH]. And it's-- it's amazing. That-- that stayed with me for God knows how many years. And then, of course, [INAUDIBLE] came. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a few weeks. I don't know dates, but we were a few weeks in the ghetto. And then, we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know when your turn came? Did they read out your names? Or did they post--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they just told us, you, you, you. And we-- we were-- so we packed up what we had, and, of course, all the clothes, you know? And we were taken to the train-- to these cattle trains-- and-- and put in. It must've been-- I don't know-- 100, 120. We were pushed in. You know, they-- they pushed us, like sardines.

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- I remember them-- them pushing us. Because it was already-- the-- the wagons were full, but they still kept pushing people in. And there was no windows, except the little, on top, with wires. I remember that because I want to-- I have to be lifted up to look, if we went through stations, what station it is, to tell them what kind of a station it is. You know? So the little ones were lifted up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and from there, the train went direct from-- from Mukacevo, the train went straight to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we go to Auschwitz, did they give you-- when they put you in the cattle cars, did they give you any food or water or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I remember any.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember them giving us food, but we had-- we took some food with us, that we had, if we had any food. No water. Water was-- because I remember, we were thirsty. I was thirsty. And everybody was screaming, water, water. And that was something that we didn't have. There was no toilet facilities. People had to do everything there. So imagine the smell. And imagine, when you are tired, a lot of people just died. So you just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you just standing? You couldn't sit?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're standing. No. No, we were-- we were just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Squashed up against--

speaker: interviewee
text: Squashed. So that was-- that was a-- then we knew already that things are not-- I mean, I knew, and then, of course, my parents would know. My elder sister knew-- she was with me, Helen. But the little ones, they were crying for food and that's-- there is nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I remember-- the station I remember vividly from the train is Cracow. I looked through and I saw Cracow. So I said, Cracow. So they said, we are in Poland. Well, from Cracow, there wasn't-- Too far.

speaker: interviewee
text: --that far from Cracow to-- to-- to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in the cattle cars for?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] It would have been a day and a night. I don't know. It could be two. Time was just-- I just lost time from-- from how long it took us to get there. But it would've been, probably, a couple of days or-- and a night, because-- or a-- or a day and a night. We arrived in Auschwitz-- we arrived in Auschwitz, I remember-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was-- times-- I would-- I would-- it was during the daytime we arrived there. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people-- did people talk in the-- in-- on the journey? Did you-- do you remember people talking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. People were crying. People were talking. They were saying, where are they taking us? What are they going to do with us? And we'll all be killed. And others said, don't talk like that. You know? You don't know. And we're going to work, and everything will be all right. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my father and mother was with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember him-- him saying anything on that train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was sort of calming us, very, very much calming us. Everything will be all right. Everything will work out. You know? And the little ones were crying, like Ella and Sura. They were little children. So we-- we were all comforting them. But we realized, I mean-- the so-called old-- older ones, we realized that it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wasn't looking good.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. So when we arrived in Auschwitz, what happened was there was selection. And my mother was with the-- with the children, you see, with the small children, like Ella, Sura, and Hersch Leib There were three of them that she had, because they were little ones. So she was with them, and Helen went-- went with her. And my father and I, we-- we were together. So, you know, it's left, right, and all that. So they put him to the right. And then they looked at me and said, left. And my father grabbed my arm to go with him. So he must have known something. And I went with him. They let me go. Because as I said, I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you were big.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very, very-- I looked big. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and then they separated us. Like, my sister went with my mother. But she was-- she was also separated from my mother and the three children because she was taller, older. And she went also on the right. And my mother went with the children straight away to the-- we know what happened after.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then they-- they gave us a shower. We took off all our clothes, all our clothes. You can imagine that then-- How many clothes you had.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they-- they sent us in to have a shower, and they shore our heads, you know, completely shorn, no hair. And they gave us the striped uniforms to put out, the-- the [GERMAN] uniform. You know? So when I put that on, I remember, mine was-- you know, because they didn't give it for small children. They didn't give it for people my age. I had a men's suit. So my father quickly rolled up my sleeves and my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Cuffs.

speaker: interviewee
text: --trousers, see, because that's all you had is the striped-- the striped uniform. That-- actually, that striped uniform, I kept till I-- till I was liberated. That was-- And then I-- I was with my father. All the men, one side, the women on the other side, you know, further up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and I don't know, somebody was watching over me. This woman walked up to me, and she asked me, how old are you? And I said, I'm 13. And she said-- quickly, she said, don't say you're 13. Say you're 17. And she was with the Nazi-- with the SS there, walking around, you know? And I said-- so I said to my father, you know, she told me to say I'm 17. And he said, say you're 17 if she told you. And that saved my life, in a way. Because they were walking around, SS, and asking, how old are you? Wie alt bist du? Everybody, they were asking, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they came to me too. Because of course I didn't look 17, when I'm shorn-- when I was without clothes, when I was in the-- in the striped uniform. So I said I was 17. He looked me up and down and up and down, and he left me there. He left me with my father.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was unbelievable. I mean, It was just luck that she walked over to me. Otherwise-- Was she walking-- telling other--

speaker: interviewee
text: She was just walking around.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did she tell other--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. She just happened to come over to me and asked me how old I was. I must've still stood out among the other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: You looked younger--

speaker: interviewee
text: For no other reason. I mean, she didn't know me. She just was walking around. And then I-- I realized that she was probably one of the friends that the Germans had, you know? Probably one of our girls, you know? Who knows. I don't know who she was, but I've been thinking a lot. She's the one that really--

speaker: interviewer
text: Saved your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: --saved my life in a way, telling me that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, probably, most of the other 13-year-olds would have been separated already and taken away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Could have been too.

speaker: interviewer
text: If it hadn't been for your father, that he grabbed you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were probably one of the youngest there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Could've been too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything your mother said when she left you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, it was such a-- such a-- everybody was crying. Everybody was holding each other. It's terrible. So then I heard my sister, that is alive, crying for water. She was-- she was among the women on the other side. And I-- I recognized her voice.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I ran around, and I asked the same woman-- I said, can I have some water please? I mean-- you know? So she-- she gave me some water. She-- she-- where she got the water from, I don't know. But she was walking around. She wasn't dressed in a-- Uniform.

speaker: interviewee
text: --uniform. Like, she was walking around with this SS, whether she was his girlfriend-- I don't know what it was. But she gave me some water. And I took the water and I ran across to where the women are. Imagine. I mean, the risk I took. Another-- another thing that I did with not even thinking. And I gave water to Helen, to my sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: And do you believe? She remembered it after the war. Even today, she still remembers how I ran across to give her water. So I'm glad I did. [LAUGHS] You're glad you gave it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So then, of course, I didn't see. The women were taken away separately, and us, the men, were taken to the barracks separately too. And I was with my father. We are the only two-- two left now. And there were some people from our town also there. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were taken to a-- to a barrack. And I don't know if you've seen. It was like a-- we were 10 or 12-- I don't remember how many. But we were also, like, put into those-- to the-- to the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Bunks. Bunks.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the banks, yes. To the banks. And in the middle, in Auschwitz, there was a-- where the-- where the Germans walked, you know, and counted you here and counted you there. So I was with my father for a few days there. And then, one day, we were Appel all the time. It's-- you-- you sort of-- they count you, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Then they call out who is going to work for here and who is going to-- to this work. And they asked, also, questions-- who was a mechanic? Who was an engineer? Who was a doctor? Who was a lawyer? You know, the things like that. So anyway, you were standing all the time, Appel, Appel, Appel, Appel. And there were some kapos, which is first time I ever heard of a kapo. They were people that were selected by the Germans to-- to keep-- to do their dirty work, you know? To hit you and to-- to-- to abuse you and to-- things like-- Keep you in line.

speaker: interviewee
text: Keep you in line, that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did they get them from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Among the people. They picked out, you know--

speaker: interviewee
text: Amongst the Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: Among the Jews. Among-- we were not-- not only with Jews there. There were other people as well there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were gypsies. There were Czech people. There were prisoners of-- you know, that are against the Germans. There was an Auschwitz-Birkenau where I was, right? And next to us was a [GERMAN] that was for-- mostly for gypsies and-- And then, of course, we had a lot of people from-- from every-- from every country in the world, there were people there. And they were giving us, already what the-- what the routine is. That if you think you're going to live here long, forget it. There are the crematoriums. And they were showing us, you know, that you only go out of Auschwitz through-- through the chimneys.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we knew. We already had a taste of it. So one day, my father was-- we were standing all in rows, and he and half the group that was standing in the row was taken to what you called the Sonderkommando. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] [SOBBING] [INAUDIBLE]. Sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Take your time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry. And so he was taken there to work. So he says to me, how much bread have you got? [SOBBING] He took his last piece of bread and gave me his. [INAUDIBLE]. [CRYING] So that was upsetting. [SOBBING] That's the last I saw him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was told that they-- they were supposed to-- to-- to sort of, burn their own people, you know? And I heard that they blew up the crematorium. That's what I've heard. I don't know how true that is, but I've heard that they [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I've never seen him again. He never came back.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just-- he left in the morning for work. And normally, they-- they come back. So it must've been true, because he never came back. So I was on my own. Then they took the-- the worst thing hurt me, that I took his piece of bread in the morning when he left. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] That stays with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he say anything to you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --when he gave you the bread?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he said, you-- I've got some. He said, you won't have enough for today. He said, and I'll see when I come back. Now, we're going to work. We kissed, and that was that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] [CRYING] I stayed in Auschwitz for a few days. And then, they were taking-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then, they were taking different blocks to go to different camps. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they asked our block to-- to [GERMAN]. That means to-- to-- to stand. We were-- we were to go to another camp. And first off, we're not even talking about the bashing and the beating in Auschwitz, still, too, that was going on. It was unbelievable. And the stench. Day and night, the crematoriums were [? working. ?] So then they said-- they-- they-- they put everybody in groups of 10-- like, 10, rows of 10.

speaker: interviewee
text: So everybody that knew each other kept together, of course. They want to go together. So-- and as I said, I didn't have a father. I didn't know anybody. Except there were some people still from our town, a few people. So I joined in that row. But they were already 10. I was 11.

speaker: interviewee
text: And still, the whole block was going. I was going too. So they marched us to the front gate. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and they say, halt! You see, they-- they count. They're very particular, the Germans. They count. Very-- every five minutes, they like to count an Appel. So they count, and there is-- the-- the line is not 10, but 11. Oh, we were--

speaker: interviewee
text: So pull me out aside, and that's when I got very, very scared because I knew if they send me back, they-- there's-- you know, they just-- I'll be-- I'll be finished, because they don't muck around there. Their aim is to-- to destroy, not to save anybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were a few other people standing also. They must've been in-- in similar situation. I noticed other people standing also there, you know, by themselves, that they picked out from the-- from the row. So whether we were five or six, they telephoned to bring in others to make up the 10. And they brought another few people and we were in rows of 10.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there-- from there, they send us to Mauthausen. So I went to-- to Camp Mauthausen. That was where the transport went. And in Mauthausen, when we arrived-- I don't remember how-- how long it took us to get there. We arrived to Mauthausen. That was in Austria. So you imagine, Auschwitz was in-- in Poland, and Mauthausen was in Austria. You went by train?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train, yes. By train. And that was a terrible camp. Oh my god, that was-- there was another [GERMAN], which was also crematoriums and gas chambers and so. So there, we stayed-- when I say 'we,' the-- it depends on what barrack you are in-- for a couple of weeks or three weeks. But that was a horrible camp. I remember it very clearly. People were carrying stones, and then, they used to just put you-- shove you into the-- to a ravine, and then you die. It was terrible. But I didn't stay there long, two or three weeks maximum.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we-- then, we were marched to Gusen. We walked from there, which is not-- obviously wasn't that far. Gusen I, it was called. And of course, each camp, as I went along, was worse than the one before. That was a-- another terrible, terrible camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And we stayed there for a few weeks, or it could be two or three months. And then, the next camp was Gusen II. We helped-- actually, they-- they build Gusen II to-- because they couldn't-- they couldn't get rid of the people fast enough by-- by gassing and killing them, so-- and all the time, the transports were coming, you know, from everywhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: So from there, we were taken to Gusen II. And we went to-- from Gusen I to Gusen II. And that's the camp where I stayed till most of-- till the-- till '45. From '44 to '45, I stayed in Gusen II.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Gusen II was just the end of the-- people that used to come from other camps told us that this is the worst camp that they've been. And there are people that have been in the camps for years over a period. In fact, there's some people here I met that have been there too. There, we were working in-- they took us to work with dogs on a-- on a-- on a-- in a place underground, in-- in a-- they call it in a-- what it-- where they were-- sort of you went into an underground and you were shoveling things on a belt--

speaker: interviewee
text: A mine.

speaker: interviewee
text: In a mine. That's it, thank you. A mine. And-- and that was a terrible camp. They were beating and belting us. Anyway, it was just terrible. And hanging people in front of you to show you, if anybody escaped-- which some of them escaped-- what they did. And we all had to watch. Whenever there's somebody that came back, you have to watch the punishment. But you couldn't, because the wires were electrified too, you know, all around us, so we couldn't-- we couldn't move.

speaker: interviewee
text: To escape.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when they took us to work in the mines, they took us with dogs. So you can imagine. We couldn't walk fast. We were hungry. We had no food. The food was just a piece of bread and-- for everybody, and a [? bit of ?] soup, which was-- which was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like-- oh, you couldn't. It was with stones and with everything. You know, if you-- if you-- but it was warm and you-- you were happy to have something in the stomach.

speaker: interviewee
text: So after-- after a while working there-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this story is something which-- I must tell you what happened to me there. We were working underground, and it was very stick-- stifling. It was hot. And-- and tired. We were tired. At night we worked. It was a-- a shocking, shocking place.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you stood with shovels and shoveling on this sand, this special sand, on a belt. You know, it was a sort of a conveyor belt. And there weren't so many guards around there. Because it was a long area, and we were lots and lots of people there from the camp. So you look around, and you see that there are not many people.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the guy that-- in-- in twos, always, you know, one for one side and the other shoveling on the stuff. So I said to the guy, you go under the belt, because it was low. You know, we dig from under the belt. And you have a little sleep. And then, after a few-- an hour or so, I'll wake you, and I'll go and have a sleep. So I-- he went and he had a sleep. I woke him up. Then, I went. And I tell you, I remember the most beautiful dream when I was at home among the family. And suddenly, I was punched. He-- he forgot about me. And it was [GERMAN], you know? Appel-- Appel. As soon as you come out of the mine, and they count you as you go in, and they count you as you go out. So of course, I copped it. What? He-- he forgot to wake you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He forgot to wake me. And they were looking.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they looked for you and found you. Oh!

speaker: interviewee
text: With dogs, oh! And the SS, they found me there. So of course, I got-- I got a terrible hiding from them. And when we came into the camp-- see, they-- they report to the camp commandants, so this is-- this is, either you get killed, shot, or whatever, or big punishment.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my punishment was I stood in front of the electrified wires for a  whole day not moving. And that was it. But the-- the bashing that I got before-- it's too horrible. Why? What did they do to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they-- they-- they-- they beat me. They hit me, and I was bleeding and everything, because they-- they, you know, with a-- with a stick, that they had to-- to go-- I mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: Looking for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's unheard of to go looking for you. So that was the experience there. And then they put-- put us, the younger ones-- because they-- they-- they-- there were other younger people. As I said, one of them is alive here, which I'll come to it. They put us in what they called [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: Potatoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Peeling potatoes-- but not peeling, but cutting potatoes in a-- in a-- in a sort of-- for the camp. And we were mostly the younger ones-- as I said, my age group-- and we must have been about-- about 20 of us there around big-- big sort of-- we were sitting around chairs, I remember, and there was water that you put it in.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, you take-- took it to a-- a big other-- like a samovar. Not a samovar. Like a-- like a bath, but huge, you know? To put the frozen potatoes so you can-- you know, your hands were frozen. Your feet were frozen. And it was cold. Oh, they were frozen that you were cutting.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. You cut-- you cut-- and it was winter. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So there was a guy called-- and that was a horrible job. It was terrible. Because they had this guy-- a kapo-- going around with a stick and, 'Schnell, schnell,' you know, to-- to work faster and faster and faster. He was just a sadist. You know? And he used to beat us on the head and used to-- it was terrible. And his name, I-- I haven't forgotten his name and I can still see his face-- his name was Bomba. Called Bomba, and he was a Spanish guy, a little Spanish fellow.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a kapo.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a kapo. And he was there for, obviously-- for-- for either-- he was a gangster or a criminal or-- well, obviously, he was, because he was this kapo and he had-- he had the right to kill if he wanted to-- whatever. And it was unbearable, because we were being hit all the time. You stay-- you sit there and you try to cut those frozen potatoes and you're being hit. And-- and there's only bone and skin, because we could hardly walk. We were that thin, you see, from-- there was no food. So this-- this little story gave us a bit of courage and, in a way, helped us. We all started to cry and pray. You see, the teaching of [HEBREW], when I was a little boy, it paid off. We all could-- all of us could pray. And for the first time, we prayed. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] He was standing, warming himself. I can still see the situation. And suddenly, he dropped dead. He had a heart attack. He dropped dead. We-- and we prayed for-- that something should happen to him. God should help us. We didn't pray for his death so much, as God should help us to-- to-- we couldn't take it anymore. Because he used to take you and, if he felt like it, and-- which he did-- and drown the person in the-- in the same-- where-- where we used to put the potatoes, he used to drown the person completely and throw them out like a sack of potatoes. That's what he used to do. So we didn't know who-- and he always used to open the window, and show us, tomorrow you go to the crematorium. And whoever he pointed to, you went. They-- they-- they were gone.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this is-- this is the guy. And the time that-- that happened was-- and I remember it very vividly-- was Hanukkah. It was-- it was Christmas at that time. Weihnachten, the Germans called it. Because we heard the-- them singing the-- the Christmas carols, the Germans-- the SS and the-- the Wehrmacht. This was Christmas, 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that was-- that was becoming to-- to-- to 1944-- Christmas, 1944. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there, of course, a commandant came and he saw the man, you know, he died. And he knew. He knew. He told us. I know he was very bad and he was tough on you and he hurt you and he killed you and he-- you know? He did all sorts of terrible things to you. From now in, it's Weihnachten. It'll be all right. The next person will be much easier on you. It lasted five minutes and, then, it's the same story. So that was-- that was one instance. But the worst thing was to-- they used to give us a shower. And then, naked, we had to stand in the snow in Appel, and-- and counting us. So you can imagine how many survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Any cruel-- cruelty that they could think of, they did. So many-- yes, sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think we'll stop the--
